skill.name: git-workflow
skill.description: Guides Git workflows with lazygit, GitHub CLI, delta, rebase, stash, bisect, worktrees, reflog, and conflict resolution commands.
skill.summary: Use guided Git and GitHub CLI workflows for staging, reviewing, rebasing, stashing, and recovering repository work.
skill.author: 0xDarkMatter

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command examples and relative worktree paths, but review shows they are documented Git workflows rather than malicious code. The skill is safe to publish with a warning because it can guide execution of powerful local and GitHub CLI operations that may rewrite history, delete stashes, discard changes, or trigger remote GitHub actions.

medium_findings.title: Powerful Git and GitHub CLI Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs use of Bash-based Git tooling, including PR merge commands, workflow runs, interactive rebase, hard reset recovery, stash deletion, and conflict operations. These commands are legitimate for the skill purpose, but mistakes can rewrite history, discard local changes, modify global Git configuration, or affect remote GitHub resources.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and semantically relevant to Git workflow assistance. The risk is operational rather than malicious because no hidden execution, obfuscation, or exfiltration path was found.

medium_findings.title: Network-Capable GitHub Operations
medium_findings.description: The skill documents GitHub CLI operations such as creating, listing, checking out, merging pull requests, viewing repositories in a browser, and running workflows. These operations require authenticated GitHub access and can change remote repository state.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file directly lists gh commands that can create or merge pull requests and run workflows. This is expected functionality, but it should be user-approved before execution.

low_findings.title: Static Command-Execution Alerts Are Mostly Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The static analyzer labeled Markdown shell examples as Ruby or shell backtick execution. The reviewed locations are fenced Bash command snippets for Git, lazygit, gh, and delta, not embedded executable code in a script.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is visible Markdown documentation with fenced bash blocks and inline command references. No Ruby code or automatic command wrapper was found.

low_findings.title: Relative Worktree Paths Are Legitimate Examples
low_findings.description: The path traversal alerts correspond to git worktree examples that place sibling worktrees in parent directories. The paths are not used for arbitrary file reads or writes by code in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are git worktree add and remove examples using ../ sibling paths. This is normal Git usage, but users should choose safe local paths.

low_findings.title: Weak-Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts appear on normal Git workflow text and stash examples. No hashing algorithm, encryption function, credential handling, or cryptographic implementation was found at those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the reported lines shows descriptions and Git stash or PR command examples, not cryptographic code. The scanner likely matched incidental text.

low_findings.title: System-Reconnaissance Alert Is Benign Git Synchronization
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance alert maps to a conflict-prevention workflow that fetches origin and rebases onto main. This checks remote Git state for normal repository synchronization.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are ordinary git fetch and rebase examples. No host enumeration, environment discovery, credential access, or external reporting was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: History Rewriting and Hard Reset Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples include interactive rebase and hard reset recovery commands. These can permanently alter working history or discard changes if used without checking the target commit.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are documented plainly and are legitimate Git operations. They are dangerous because misuse affects repository state, not because malicious intent is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Repository State Changes
dangerous_patterns.description: GitHub CLI examples can create pull requests, check out pull requests, merge pull requests, and run workflows. These actions can affect shared repositories and CI systems.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The gh commands are direct and require authenticated GitHub context. They are expected for this skill but warrant user confirmation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Data-Loss-Prone Stash Cleanup
dangerous_patterns.description: Stash examples include dropping a stash, clearing all stashes, and discarding conflicted working tree changes. These commands can remove recoverable work.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in the documentation and can delete temporary work. The risk is user-error impact rather than hidden behavior.

content.value_statement: Git work can become risky when staging, rebasing, stashing, and reviewing happen across many branches. This skill gives Codex, Claude, and Claude Code structured command guidance for safer Git and GitHub CLI workflows.
actual_capabilities: Provides quick references for lazygit, gh, delta, and common Git commands.
Guides pull request creation, review, checkout, merge, issue, repository, and workflow commands with GitHub CLI.
Documents interactive rebase workflows for squashing, reordering, editing, splitting, and autosquashing commits.
Explains stash operations for saving, applying, dropping, branching, and resolving stash conflicts.
Covers advanced Git workflows including bisect, cherry-pick, worktrees, reflog recovery, and conflict resolution.
Shows when to load deeper reference files for rebase, stash, and advanced Git patterns.
limitations: Requires git, gh, lazygit, and delta to be installed separately.
GitHub operations require authenticated gh access and network availability.
The skill gives command guidance, but it does not validate repository policy or branch protection rules.
Destructive Git commands still require careful review before execution.

use_cases.title: Review and Ship Pull Requests
use_cases.description: Create, inspect, check out, and merge pull requests from the terminal using GitHub CLI guidance.

use_cases.title: Clean Feature Branch History
use_cases.description: Use interactive rebase patterns to squash, reorder, edit, split, and autosquash commits before review.

use_cases.title: Recover and Diagnose Git Problems
use_cases.description: Use bisect, reflog, cherry-pick, worktrees, and conflict workflows to isolate bugs and recover work.

prompt_templates.title: Check My Git Status
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me inspect my current Git state. Explain what to check before staging, committing, pulling, or pushing.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Pull Request
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through creating a GitHub pull request from my current branch using gh. Ask before running any command that changes remote state.

prompt_templates.title: Clean Up Commits
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me clean this feature branch with interactive rebase. Identify which commits to squash, reword, or keep before any rewrite command runs.

prompt_templates.title: Diagnose a Regression
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide a git bisect session to find the commit that introduced this regression. Include reset and recovery steps before starting.

output_examples.input: I need to review changes and open a pull request.
output: A short preflight checklist for branch status, staged files, and remote tracking.
Suggested gh commands for creating or opening a pull request.
A reminder to confirm title, body, target branch, and remote effects before execution.

output_examples.input: My branch has many small fix commits before review.
output: A rebase plan that separates commits to keep, squash, reword, or drop.
Safety checks for public branch status and reflog recovery.
Step-by-step guidance for continuing or aborting the rebase.

output_examples.input: I lost work after a reset or rebase.
output: A recovery path using reflog to locate the previous commit.
Options for creating a recovery branch before changing state again.
Warnings about commands that may discard additional work.

best_practices: Check status, branch, and remote target before running commands that rewrite history or affect GitHub.
Create a backup branch before rebase, hard reset, stash cleanup, or recovery work.
Review diffs with delta or Git before staging, committing, merging, or applying a stash.
anti_patterns: Running hard reset, stash clear, or checkout discard commands without confirming recoverability.
Rebasing shared public branches without team agreement or protected branch awareness.
Merging pull requests or running workflows through gh without confirming repository, branch, and permissions.

faq.question: Does this skill execute Git commands automatically?
faq.answer: It provides command guidance for Bash-capable assistants. Users should review and approve state-changing commands before execution.

faq.question: Which tools are required?
faq.answer: The skill expects git, gh, lazygit, and delta. GitHub actions require gh authentication and network access.

faq.question: Can it help with pull requests?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes gh commands for creating, listing, viewing, checking out, and merging pull requests.

faq.question: Can it help recover lost commits?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents reflog workflows for deleted branches, failed rebases, hard resets, and dangling commits.

faq.question: Is it safe for shared branches?
faq.answer: Use caution. Rebase and reset commands can rewrite history, so shared branches need explicit review first.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk level medium?
faq.answer: The reviewed files are not malicious, but the skill guides commands that can change local and remote repository state.
